summary: Gruber revisits the widespread predictions from two weeks prior that Twitter would technically collapse following mass layoffs and resignations under Elon Musk. He compiles quotes from Bloomberg, The Washington Post, The Verge, and The Guardian, all featuring engineers and insiders predicting imminent system failures. With the World Cup driving record usage and Twitter still functioning, Gruber notes these fears appear overblown. He hedges slightly, acknowledging the World Cup is only half over. He closes by pivoting to what is actually collapsing: Twitter's advertising business.
key_points: Multiple major outlets quoted engineers predicting Twitter's imminent technical collapse after mass layoffs gutted critical infrastructure teams
Two weeks later, with World Cup traffic stress-testing the platform, Twitter remained operational — contradicting the dire predictions
The predicted 50% chance of a major World Cup outage had not materialized halfway through the tournament
While technical collapse fears were overblown, Twitter's advertising business was genuinely collapsing with advertisers fleeing the platform
money_quotes: Two weeks later — with World Cup soccer drama fueling record usage — such concerns seem to have been overblown.
Two weeks later and it seems they can run Twitter without that team.
Or, perhaps, it's just been luck and collapse is imminent.
But while fears of technical collapse seem to have been overblown, Twitter's advertising collapse is seemingly continuing unabated.
The World Cup is only half over. Let's check back in another two weeks.
key_insight: The tech press amplified insider predictions of Twitter's imminent technical collapse that didn't materialize, but the real crisis — advertiser flight — received far less dramatic coverage.
